title: Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023
jurisdiction: England, Scotland and Wales (does NOT apply in Northern Ireland)
chapters: 3 sections. A short but transformative Act that amends the Equality Act 2010 by inserting a new s.40A — imposing a POSITIVE, PROACTIVE duty on employers to take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment of their employees. This shifts the legal framework from REACTIVE (defending after an incident) to ANTICIPATORY (preventing incidents before they occur). The Act also amends the EqA 2010 s.124 to allow employment tribunals to uplift compensation by up to 25% where the employer has breached the preventative duty.

parliamentaryOrigin.bill: Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Bill [Private Members' Bill]
parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Wera Hobhouse MP (Liberal Democrat) — Private Members' Bill. The Bill originally included a reinstatement of third-party harassment liability and required 'all reasonable steps' rather than 'reasonable steps'. Both provisions were removed during parliamentary passage to secure Government support.
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: In force from 26 October 2024. One year between Royal Assent and commencement to allow employers to prepare. The EHRC published updated technical guidance on 26 September 2024 — one month before commencement.

significantAmendments.instrument: Employment Rights Act 2025 (proposed amendments — anticipated commencement 2026-2027)
significantAmendments.effect: The ERA 2025 proposes to: (1) upgrade the duty from 'reasonable steps' to 'ALL reasonable steps' (anticipated October 2026), (2) give the Secretary of State power to define 'reasonable steps' by regulations (anticipated 2027), (3) reintroduce direct employer liability for THIRD-PARTY harassment across ALL protected characteristics, (4) make sexual harassment a specified protected disclosure (whistleblowing protection). These amendments will significantly strengthen the duty when they come into force.

precedingDocuments.title: Equality Act 2010 s.26 (existing harassment provisions)
precedingDocuments.significance: The existing harassment framework. Sexual harassment is defined as unwanted conduct of a sexual nature that has the purpose or effect of violating dignity or creating an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating, or offensive environment. The Worker Protection Act ADDS a preventative duty on top of this existing framework.

precedingDocuments.title: Equality Act 2010 s.109 (employer's vicarious liability and 'all reasonable steps' defence)
precedingDocuments.significance: Under s.109, an employer is vicariously liable for harassment by employees UNLESS the employer can show they took 'all reasonable steps' to prevent it. The Worker Protection Act creates a SEPARATE, ADDITIONAL preventative duty — breach of the preventative duty and failure of the s.109 defence are DIFFERENT legal tests.

precedingDocuments.title: EHRC Technical Guidance: Sexual Harassment and Harassment at Work (updated 26 September 2024)
precedingDocuments.significance: The authoritative EHRC guidance on the preventative duty. Includes the 8-step employer action plan. While not legally binding, the guidance sets out the EHRC's expectations and is likely to be treated as a benchmark by employment tribunals.

leadingCases.name: No reported case law as at March 2026 — the duty is less than 18 months old
leadingCases.principle: The preventative duty came into force on 26 October 2024. As at March 2026, no employment tribunal decisions on the 25% compensation uplift have been reported. EHRC enforcement action has been taken (unlawful act notices and legally binding agreements) but no published precedents exist. SURVEILLANCE: monitor for the first tribunal decisions applying the uplift and the first EHRC enforcement actions.
leadingCases.significance: Sex discrimination claims rose from 457 (2023/24) to 1,375 (2024/25) — a threefold increase coinciding with the commencement of the preventative duty. The first tribunal decisions applying the 25% uplift will be critical for establishing what 'reasonable steps' means in practice.

parts.title: Part 1 — The Preventative Duty: What Employers Must Do

sections.num: EqA 2010 s.40A(1) (inserted by Worker Protection Act 2023 s.1)
sections.title: The positive, proactive duty to take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment
sections.summary: The Worker Protection Act inserts a new s.40A(1) into the Equality Act 2010: 'An employer (A) must take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment of employees of A in the course of their employment.' This is a POSITIVE duty — it applies to ALL employers regardless of whether any complaint has been made. It is PROACTIVE — it requires the employer to ANTICIPATE risks and ACT before harassment occurs. It is ONGOING — compliance is not a one-off exercise but a continuous obligation to assess, plan, implement, monitor, and review. The EHRC has enforcement powers to take action even where NO incident of sexual harassment has taken place — the duty exists independently of any complaint.
sections.text:
THE DUTY — s.40A(1):

'An employer must take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment of employees in the course of their employment.'

KEY CHARACTERISTICS:
(1) POSITIVE: the duty exists regardless of whether any sexual harassment has occurred or been reported.
(2) PROACTIVE: the employer must anticipate where sexual harassment might occur and take steps to prevent it BEFORE it happens.
(3) ONGOING: this is not a one-off compliance exercise. The employer must continuously assess risks, implement measures, monitor effectiveness, and adapt.
(4) COVERS THIRD-PARTY HARASSMENT: the EHRC guidance states that the duty 'requires employers to take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment of workers by third parties' — customers, clients, visitors, contractors. While the Act itself removed the standalone third-party liability provision, the EHRC treats third-party prevention as part of the preventative duty.
(5) EXTENDS BEYOND THE WORKPLACE: covers work events, conferences, drinks, Christmas parties, client entertainment — any situation 'in the course of employment'.

WHAT IS 'SEXUAL HARASSMENT':
Unwanted conduct of a sexual nature that has the purpose or effect of:
(a) violating a person's dignity, OR
(b) creating an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating, or offensive environment.

Includes: unwanted sexual comments, jokes, or banter; unwanted physical contact; sexual advances or propositions; displaying sexually explicit material; sending sexual messages or images; sexual assault.

WHAT ARE 'REASONABLE STEPS':
The EHRC guidance makes clear that what is 'reasonable' depends on the facts and circumstances. Relevant factors include:
(a) The size and resources of the employer.
(b) The nature of the working environment (customer-facing roles carry higher third-party risk).
(c) The sector (hospitality, retail, healthcare — higher interaction with third parties).
(d) Whether concerns have previously been raised.
(e) The likely effect of taking a particular step.
(f) Whether steps already taken have been effective.

EHRC 8-STEP EMPLOYER ACTION PLAN:
(1) Develop and communicate a robust anti-harassment policy that explicitly covers sexual harassment AND third-party harassment.
(2) Undertake regular risk assessments — identify WHERE sexual harassment may occur (work events, lone working, client-facing roles, night shifts) and WHAT steps will prevent it.
(3) Engage with staff — use 1-2-1s, surveys, exit interviews, focus groups to understand the reality of the workplace culture.
(4) Provide training — tailored, not generic. Cover: what sexual harassment is, how to report it, bystander intervention, manager responsibilities. Repeat regularly, not once.
(5) Establish clear reporting channels — multiple routes (line manager, HR, whistleblowing hotline, external helpline). Employees must feel safe to report without fear of reprisal.
(6) Respond effectively to complaints — investigate promptly, take appropriate action, support the complainant. A complaint that is poorly handled is evidence that the duty has not been met.
(7) Monitor and evaluate — track complaints, survey results, training completion, exit interview data. Look for patterns and trends.
(8) Review and update — the risk assessment and action plan must be living documents, reviewed at least annually and whenever a significant change occurs.

THE 25% COMPENSATION UPLIFT (s.124 EqA 2010, as amended):
If an employee brings a successful claim for sexual harassment under the EqA 2010, the employment tribunal MAY increase the compensation by up to 25% if it finds that the employer has breached the s.40A(1) preventative duty. This is an UPLIFT, not a standalone award — there must first be a successful harassment claim.

EHRC ENFORCEMENT:
(1) The EHRC can take enforcement action for breach of the preventative duty even where NO incident of sexual harassment has taken place.
(2) Enforcement powers include: investigations, unlawful act notices, legally binding agreements (requiring the employer to implement an action plan), and court injunctions.
(3) Workers can report concerns directly to the EHRC.
(4) The EHRC has demonstrated willingness to intervene — enforcement activity has commenced since October 2024.
sections.keyPrinciple: This Act represents a fundamental philosophical shift in employment law: from reactive to proactive, from defending AFTER harm to preventing BEFORE harm. The employer who waits for a complaint before acting is already in breach. The employer who has a policy but has never risk-assessed, trained, or monitored is already in breach. The employer who has done all of those things for employee-on-employee harassment but has not considered third-party risks is already in breach. The bar is not perfection — it is REASONABLE STEPS, genuinely and continuously implemented.
refs: EqA 2010 s.40A(1) (preventative duty — inserted by WPA 2023 s.1)
EqA 2010 s.26 (definition of harassment and sexual harassment)
EqA 2010 s.109 (vicarious liability and 'all reasonable steps' defence — separate from s.40A)
EqA 2010 s.124 (compensation — 25% uplift for breach of preventative duty)
EHRC Technical Guidance: Sexual Harassment and Harassment at Work (updated September 2024)
ERA 2025 (proposed amendments: 'all reasonable steps', third-party liability, regulations defining 'reasonable steps')

regulatoryGuidance.title: Sexual harassment and harassment at work: Technical Guidance (updated September 2024)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The authoritative EHRC guidance on the preventative duty. Includes the 8-step action plan, risk assessment methodology, and worked examples of compliance and non-compliance. Essential reading for every HR manager.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Sexual harassment at work
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on preventing and handling sexual harassment, including training resources and policy templates.

commonErrors: Treating the preventative duty as a one-off compliance exercise — 'we updated the policy in October 2024, job done'. The duty is ONGOING. Risk assessments must be reviewed, training must be repeated, monitoring must be continuous, and the action plan must be updated

Having an anti-harassment policy but not training managers on how to handle complaints — a policy without training is paper compliance. The EHRC expects tailored, regular training that covers what sexual harassment is, how to report it, bystander intervention, and manager responsibilities

Not conducting a specific sexual harassment risk assessment — a generic H&S risk assessment is not sufficient. The employer must identify WHERE sexual harassment risks arise in their specific workplace: work events, client meetings, lone working, night shifts, power imbalances, vulnerable workers

Ignoring third-party harassment risk — the EHRC treats the preventative duty as covering harassment by customers, clients, visitors, and contractors. An employer in hospitality, retail, healthcare, or any customer-facing sector who has not assessed third-party risk is in breach

Not monitoring the effectiveness of steps taken — implementing measures is only half the duty. The employer must also CHECK whether those measures are working: are complaints being made? Are staff surveys showing improvement? Are exit interviews revealing ongoing problems?

Relying on the absence of complaints as evidence of compliance — the absence of complaints may indicate that employees do not feel safe to report, not that harassment is not occurring. The EHRC expects the employer to proactively engage with staff to understand the reality

Not establishing multiple reporting channels — employees must have options beyond reporting to their line manager (who may be the perpetrator). An anonymous helpline, an external whistleblowing channel, or a designated harassment officer provides alternative routes

Failing to take action when complaints are upheld — investigating a complaint but taking no meaningful action against the perpetrator undermines the entire framework and is evidence of non-compliance with the preventative duty

Not understanding the relationship between the preventative duty (s.40A) and the vicarious liability defence (s.109) — they are SEPARATE legal tests. An employer can breach the preventative duty even if they would succeed on the s.109 defence, and vice versa

pendingAmendments.instrument: ERA 2025 — upgrade to 'all reasonable steps' (anticipated October 2026)
pendingAmendments.status: The ERA 2025 proposes upgrading the duty from 'reasonable steps' to 'all reasonable steps'. This is a higher standard — case law on the s.109 vicarious liability defence shows that 'all reasonable steps' requires a more comprehensive response. CRITICAL: employers should prepare for the higher standard NOW rather than waiting for commencement.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — significant increase in employer obligation

pendingAmendments.status: The ERA 2025 proposes reintroducing direct employer liability for third-party harassment across all protected characteristics. This will create a standalone right for employees to claim against their employer where a customer, client, or visitor harasses them and the employer has not taken all reasonable steps to prevent it.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — creates new standalone cause of action for employees

pendingAmendments.instrument: First tribunal decisions on the 25% uplift
pendingAmendments.status: As at March 2026, no reported decisions on the uplift. Monitor for the first cases which will establish judicial interpretation of what constitutes 'reasonable steps' and the circumstances in which the uplift is applied.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — will define the practical standard for all employers
